name: Sarter, Nadine B.
recent_1: Flanigen, P., Wilson, M., Sarter, N., & Atkins, E. (2024). The effectiveness of sensor visualizations and graphic augmentations for detecting vertical obstacles. Journal of the American Helicopter Society, 69(3), 1-13. DOI: 10.4050/jahs.69.032011
recent_1_synopsis: This study evaluated how different sensor visualizations and graphic augmentations help helicopter pilots detect vertical obstacles such as power lines and towers. It informs the design of obstacle-warning displays that support timely detection in degraded-visibility rotorcraft operations.
recent_2: Mishler, S., & Sarter, N. (2022). Attention limitations in the detection and identification of alarms in close temporal proximity. Human Factors, 66(1), 234-249. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211063991
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how well operators detect and identify alarms that occur close together in time, a common challenge in alarm-rich settings. It documents attentional bottlenecks that cause near-simultaneous alarms to be missed or confused, with implications for alarm system design.
recent_3: Cutlip, S., Wan, Y., Sarter, N., & Gillespie, R. B. (2021). The effects of haptic feedback and transition type on transfer of control between drivers and vehicle automation. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 51(6), 613-621. DOI: 10.1109/thms.2021.3107255
recent_3_synopsis: This study tested how haptic feedback and different transition types affect handovers of control between drivers and vehicle automation. It shows that touch-based cues can smooth and improve the safety of control transfers in automated driving.
seminal_1: Sarter, N. B., & Woods, D. D. (1995). How in the world did we ever get into that mode? Mode error and awareness in supervisory control. Human Factors, 37(1), 5-19. DOI: 10.1518/001872095779049516
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark paper introduced the concepts of mode error and mode awareness, explaining why operators of complex automated systems (especially glass-cockpit aircraft) experience 'automation surprises.' With well over 1,000 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is a foundational work on human-automation interaction and the gulf between operators' mental models and system behavior.
seminal_2: Jones, L. A., & Sarter, N. B. (2008). Tactile displays: Guidance for their design and application. Human Factors, 50(1), 90-111. DOI: 10.1518/001872008x250638
seminal_2_synopsis: This review provides design guidance for tactile (touch-based) information displays, synthesizing perception research to advise when and how to use the sense of touch in human-machine interfaces. With well over 1,000 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2025), it is a foundational reference that helped establish tactile and multimodal displays as a mainstream interface modality.
expertise_overview: Human-automation interaction and the cognitive and attentional foundations of interface design. Studies automation surprises, mode errors, and mode awareness, and how transparency and feedback help operators understand and trust complex automated systems. A leading authority on multimodal and tactile information displays, examining how visual, auditory, and tactile channels can be combined to support attention allocation, alarm detection, and human-automation coordination, including crossmodal matching of stimulus intensities. Investigates attention limitations, change blindness, age-related differences in multisensory perception, and trust calibration, often inferred through eye tracking. Application domains span aviation and rotorcraft flight decks, automated driving, and safety-critical monitoring, using simulator experiments and process-oriented behavioral measures.
notes: Identified with very high confidence: Nadine B. Sarter is the Richard W. Pew Collegiate Professor of Industrial & Operations Engineering at the University of Michigan and a National Academy of Engineering member (2019); confirmed via Wikipedia, Research.com, her Google Scholar profile, and authorship on all four seed hf_papers. Citation counts are approximate, from Google Scholar around mid-2025; the two seminal papers each exceed 1,000 citations (the 1995 mode-error paper is among the most-cited works in human factors). All five cited DOIs verified resolving via the Crossref API (June 2026) with matching titles; the four seed DOIs were pre-verified. Crossref returns multiple unrelated 'Sarter' authors (public administration, chemistry); the cited recent papers were confirmed to be Nadine Sarter's by co-authors (Gillespie, Atkins, Lu) and aviation/automation topic. Note Sage online-publication years differ from issue years (the 2022-online alarm paper appears in vol. 66, 2024 issue).
